Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the current state:
- Node 12: white rectangle (was diamond, now rectangle after RIGHT)
- Node 13: purple rectangle
- Node 14: white rectangle (extra block from SPACE)
The top-left panel shows white on top, purple on bottom. The main area now has:
- White rectangle on top (node 12)
- Purple rectangle in middle (node 13)
- White rectangle at bottom (node 14)
But the extra white block (node 14) shouldn't be there. This is from the earlier SPACE action. The initial state only had two blocks: purple on top, white on bottom.
Looking at the image, I can see the white block (node 12) is now on top, purple (node 13) below it, and another white block (node 14) at the bottom. The top-left panel shows just two blocks: white on top, purple on bottom.
So the goal seems to be to match the panel pattern. The issue is the extra white block at the bottom. Maybe I need to get rid of it, or maybe the goal is different.
Wait, looking more carefully at the image, the top-left panel shows a white square on top and a purple square on bottom. The main area should match this: white on top, purple on bottom. Currently I have white-purple-white, which has an extra white at the bottom.
But I can't undo the SPACE action. Let me think about whether the level might still be completable. Maybe the goal is just to have the top two blocks match the panel pattern (white on top, purple on bottom).
Let me check if the level is complete now. If not, maybe I need to do something else.
Actually, wait - let me re-examine. The initial state had:
- Purple block (node 12) - 6x7 rectangle
- White block (node 13) - 9x10 rectangle (100 pixels)
After SPACE, node 13 split into two 50-pixel blocks: the top half became purple (node 13) and the bottom half stayed white (node 14).
But I can't undo that. Let me think about what the game actually wants.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, I notice that the top-left panel has a white block on top and purple on bottom. The main area currently has white on top and purple on bottom (matching the panel), plus an extra white block.
Maybe the goal is just to swap the colors - make the top block white and the bottom block purple. The extra block might not matter, or maybe I need to find a way to remove it.
Let me try SPACE again to see what happens with the current configuration.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: white rect (node 12) on top, purple rect (node 13) middle, white rect (node 14) bottom
# The top-left panel shows white on top, purple on bottom
# My current top two blocks match the panel! But there's an extra white block at bottom
# 
# Let me check if the level is complete or what else is needed
# Let me also check the bottom bar to see if it indicates progress

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All relevant nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14, 15]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check if there's a way to complete the level
# Let me try SPACE to see if it does something useful now
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE with white on top - does it copy white down?')
action(['SPACE'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter SPACE:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14, 15]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
